text:
On the Dry Bulk side, activity has remained notably strong, more than a handful transactions were concluded this week across a broad range of segments,from Handysize up to Post-Panamaxes. Buyers' appetite remains healthy, underpinning a consistently active dry sale and purchase market.
On the Tanker side, activity has been comparatively more selective, in line with the previous weeks.
At the same time, market participants are closely monitoring geopolitical developments following the signing of the Memorandum of Agreement between the US and Iran. While the agreement has been welcomed as a step towards de-escalation, the longer-term implications remain uncertain, and the market awaits to see greater clarity on the normalization in the Strait of Hormuz during the following weeks.
Dry Cargo
In the Post-Panamax segment, Greek buyers emerged behind the scrubber-fitted CORNELIE OLDENDORFF (ABT 93K DWT, 2011, ZHEJIANG, SCRUBBER), which is understood sold in the high USD 15m range, with delivery in October.
On the Kamsarmax front, Japanese Owners concluded the sale of the MONT BLANC HAWK (ABT 82K DWT, 2017, IMABARI) after inviting offers earlier in the week, achieving a price in the region of USD 32.5m.
The Supramax AFRICAN TERN (ABT 58K DWT, 2013, DALIAN COSCO) has been sold for high USD 19m, whilst the SANTA RITA (ABT 56K DWT, 2010, MITSUI) was acquired by Greek interests for USD 17.2m.
In the Handysize sector, c/o Devbulk have acquired the SHINSUNG ACCORD (ABT 37K DWT, 2015, SAIKI) and the ASTRO PROPUS (ABT 38K DWT, 2014, IMABARI) at USD 19m each. Separately, the DEVBULK IMABARI (ABT 29K DWT, 2009, IMABARI, OHBS/GC) has been sold to Turkish buyers for USD 10.5m.
Tanker
On the Aframax segment, the SAMOS (ABT 105K DWT, 2010, SUMITOMO) achieved a price around region USD mid-40m

Tanker
On the Aframax segment, the SAMOS (ABT 105K DWT, 2010, SUMITOMO) achieved a price around region USD mid-40m's basis Q3 delivery.
Elsewhere, the MR SANDPIPER PACIFIC (ABT 51K DWT, 2013, SUNGDONG) was sold for USD 32m.
(All details given in good faith and for information only, without guarantee/prejudice)

Capesize Rates Remain Seasonally Strong Despite Recent Drop - The dry bulk market continues to demonstrate
Source: Breakwave Advisors
snippet: Capesize Rates Remain Seasonally Strong Despite Recent Drop - The dry bulk market continues to demonstrate Source: Breakwave Advisors

text:
The US-Iran ceasefire has not yet changed much on the water, while the impact of the signing of the Memorandum of Agreement betweenthe US and Iran on the Hormuz chokepoint remains to be seen. For now, uncertainty surrounding transit through the Strait persists, with the number of monitored transits still well below pre-conflict levels.
In dry bulk, rates softened with the recording a week-on-week decline of approximately 7.3%. On tankers, in contrast to the rate easing seen in previous weeks, a slight increase was observed this week, particularly across the larger tanker segments.
Overall, we observed healthy S&P activity this week in both the Dry and Wet markets, despite fluctuations in freight rates. On Dry, while demand for larger tonnage (Kamsarmaxes through to Capes) remains, activity this week mainly concentrated on geared tonnage ranging from Handysize up to Ultramax vessels. On the tanker side, while enquiry for tonnage has been maintained, buyers are adopting a more cautious approach in light of the ongoing geopolitical developments
Dry Cargo
The Kamsarmax SEACON HAMBURG (ABT 86K DWT, 2023, HUANGPU WENCHONG) has been committed at USD 37.5m with a time charter attached until February-June 2028. For comparison, the same aged sister vessels SEACON VANCOUVER and SEACON OSLO were sold two months ago for USD 36.35m each basis a charter free delivery between October - November 2026.
On the Ultramax front, the ULTRA SAKA (ex-name MITSOS - ABT 64K DWT, 2013, CHENGXI), was acquired by Chinese interests for USD 23.25m. Interestingly, current sellers bought the MITSOS, only a few months back at region USD 20.5m.
On the Supramax size, the Japanese-built OCEAN BRIGHT (ABT 56K DWT, 2013, MITSUI) was sold for xs USD 19m, whereas the LILA CUMBERLAND (ABT 56K DWT, 2013, JIANGSU HANTONG) was sold in the high USD 16's m levels to c/o ADNOC L&S.
The Handysize DARYA KRISHNA (ABT 34K DWT,

alongside expanded export programs from ADNOC, Qatar, Iraq, Kuwait, and Iran.

snippet: Source asset: reports/hellenic/shipbuilding/pdfs/2026-06-30_breakwave-tanker-report-6-30-2026_breakwavetankersjune302026report_19efd5a76b05.pdf  [Page 1] Tanker Shipping Bi-Weekly Industry Report June

text:
30D: -26.6% ↓
rapid shift in oil market sentiment from supply anxiety to an oversupply narrative has
YTD: 318.8% ↑
driven crude prices back to pre-war levels, despite little change in structural reality.
While global inventories continue a rapid drawdown and uncertainty persists over YOY: 412.1% ↑
the pace of Arabian Gulf (AG) export recovery, a steep decline in Chinese spot
market purchasing has put severe downward pressure on global pricing. The
primary driver of this deflation is not necessarily the volume of barrels transiting the Reuters: BWET.K
Hormuz straits (which is admittedly high in such a short period of time), but rather Bloomberg: BWET US
Exchange: NYSE
the notable lack of appetite from Chinese refiners to accumulate stockpiles at pre-
conflict rates. Whether this pullback represents a structural shift in Chinese demand
(economy) or a temporary buying pause remains to be seen; however, the
Breakwave Advisors Research
immediate seaborne market is left fundamentally imbalanced with a surplus of research@breakwaveadvisors.com
prompt spot oil supply.
• Our Long-term View - The tanker market has been recovering from a long period
of staggered rates as the growth in new vessel supply shrunk while oil demand
remained elevated in line with the global economy. The recent rapid increase in
freight rates has led to significant new vessel ordering, with the orderbook now
standing at well above average levels, and although in the near term such a
supply/demand misbalance is small, we expect a meaningful negative balance to
develop longer term leading to a industry downcycle.

The Baltic TD3C Index measures the spot rate in USD per ton for Very Large Crude Carriers (VLLCC) operating in the Middle East to Asia route. The Breakwave
Tanker Futures Index (BDRYFF) is designed to track freight futures contracts with a sector weighting of 90% VLCC TD3C and 10% Suezmax TD20 and a
weighted average maturity of approximately 50-70 days.

Fund, and fluctuations in price could materially affect the Funds' shares. Investments in freight futures typically fluctuate in value with changes in spot charter rates.
Charter rates for dry bulk and tanker vessels are volatile and have declined significantly since their historic highs and may remain at low levels or decrease further
in the future. The Funds will not take defensive positions to protect against declining freight rates, which could cause a decline to the value of the Funds' shares.
